title: Fitting a door viewer (peephole) into a front door
subtitle: Drill and fit a wide-angle door viewer so you can see who is outside without opening the door
excerpt: How to drill and fit a wide-angle door viewer (peephole) into a solid timber or composite front door. Covers choosing the right height, drilling the through-hole, and assembling the two-part viewer body.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 3621: Thief resistant locks. Master Locksmith Association installation guidance. General trade practice (door hardware installation).

glossaryTerms.definition: An optical lens assembly that fits through a hole in a door and allows the occupant to see a wide-angle view of the area outside without opening the door. Standard body diameter is 14 mm; most viewers accept doors from 35 mm to 55 mm thick. Wide-angle viewers cover 200 degrees of field; narrower viewers cover 160 degrees. The viewer body is two threaded pieces that screw together through the hole — the outer lens (the one visible from outside) and the inner eyepiece.

content.text: fits through a 14 mm hole in the door. The job requires a cordless drill, a 14 mm wood (flat) bit or a step drill, and 20 minutes. The only decision that matters before drilling is the height: it should be at eye level for the adults who will use it; typically 1400 to 1500 mm from the floor. On a composite door, check the manufacturer's data: some composite core materials require a different approach.

A wide-angle door viewer (standard 14 mm diameter body, rated for the door thickness; verify before purchase). A 14 mm flat (spade) wood bit or step drill bit. A cordless drill. A tape measure. A combination square. A pencil. The small hex tool or grub-screw key sometimes included with the viewer (check the pack).

Measure and mark the centre of the door horizontally; this is where the hole will be drilled, centred on the door edge-to-edge. Mark the height: measure up from the floor to the eye level of the person who will use it most often. A standard recommendation of 1450 mm suits most adults but should be adjusted for a household where the tallest or most frequent user differs significantly from that height. Mark clearly with a pencil cross.
Step 2, drill from both faces to avoid breakout

Fit the 14 mm flat bit. Drill from the outside (front) face of the door, drilling slowly until the tip of the bit just breaks through to the inside face. Stop immediately when the tip appears; do not drill the full hole from one side. Come around to the inside face and drill the remaining material from the inside face through to meet the first bore. This two-pass technique prevents the spade bit from tearing a rough breakout on the exit face of the door, which would be visible and unsightly.

Insert the outer lens barrel through the hole from the outside face of the door. Screw the inner eyepiece onto the outer barrel thread from the inside, hand-tightening until the viewer bodies are snug against both door faces. Do not overtighten with a tool; the lens housings are usually aluminium or brass and the thread strips easily. The viewer is now ready to use. Look through the eyepiece: you should have a clear, wide-angle view through the outer lens. If the view is partially obscured, the lens assembly is not centred in the hole; loosen, reposition, and retighten.

attrs.symptom: Breakout on the door face around the hole
attrs.cause: Flat bit was drilled all the way through from one side at too high a speed.
attrs.fix: Fill the breakout with a two-part wood filler matching the door colour. Sand smooth when cured. On new work, always drill from both faces.

attrs.symptom: View through the viewer is dark or partially blocked
attrs.cause: Viewer is not rated for the door thickness — the barrel is too short for the door depth.
attrs.fix: Return the viewer and purchase one rated for the correct door thickness. Door thickness varies: measure before purchasing.
